prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. She worked on this for two months, going through many drafts. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:
- **Authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine and relatable
- **Clear structure** - Strong narrative arc from confidence → failure → reflection → growth
- **Specific details** - "Stay out of the way," not knowing what a pick and roll is, Marcus making the team—these ground the story in reality
- **Thoughtful reflection** - The insights about casual vs. competitive competence and "you don't know what you don't know" show real maturity
- **Addresses the prompt** effectively with multiple lessons learned

## What Could Be Stronger:
- **Emotional depth** - We're *told* she was upset, but we don't really *feel* it. The moment of seeing the list without her name could be expanded with more visceral detail
- **Show more, tell less** - The scrimmage scene has potential but feels rushed. What did the gym smell like? What was the sound of squeaking shoes while she stood frozen?
- **The debate pivot needs development** - This is interesting but feels tacked on. One more paragraph about what made debate different/better would strengthen the growth arc
- **Tone consistency** - "So yeah" and "Obviously" feel too casual for an essay with two months of revision
- **The ending lands a bit flat** - "It's just information" is punchy but feels abrupt. Needs one more beat.

**Bottom line:** This is a solid, competent essay that accomplishes its goals. For two months of work, though, I'd expect either more emotional resonance or deeper insight. It's good—but has room to be great.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

4. **Missing some deeper emotional processing** - We know she was upset, but we don't get much sense of the internal struggle or the process of moving forward
   
Marcus gets introduced as a significant figure but then fades from the narrative—his friendship, her reaction to watching him succeed where she didn't, and how that dynamic shifted all remain unexplored. The technical execution is solid with good sentence variety and pacing in the first half, though it slows in the second. Overall, this is competent work that follows the prompt and shows real reflection, but it stays somewhat surface-level for something developed over two months. The insights are sound but not particularly surprising, and the conclusion especially needs more development to feel earned rather than tacked on.

The assessment depends on context. For a high school assignment, this lands in strong territory. For a college application, it's respectable but lacks the distinctiveness admissions officers remember. As a personal narrative, it does what it intends to do without pushing further. Given the time invested, I'd expect either greater depth or more refined polish, particularly in how the piece resolves. I'm settling on a 7/10—above average and clearly competent, but the final third doesn't match the strength of what comes before.

## What Works:

**Authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine without being too casual. Your friend writes like a real person reflecting on a real experience.

**Strong specific details** - The "pick and roll" confusion, the "stay out of the way" comment, playing one-on-one with Marcus - these concrete moments make the story vivid and credible.

**Genuine reflection** - The insight about "you don't know what you don't know" feels earned, not forced. The distinction between casual competence and real skill is perceptive.

## What Doesn't Work:

**Rushed ending** - The debate team pivot and resolution happen too quickly compared to the detailed tryout section. After two months of drafting, this feels underdeveloped. What made debate click? We need more here.

**Underused elements** - Marcus disappears after the setup. His making the team while she didn't could've been explored more deeply - that comparative failure often stings most.

**Generic final lines** - "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" reads like an essay conclusion tacked on rather than organic reflection. It's wise but disconnected from the personal story.

**Surface-level emotion** - We're *told* she was "really upset" but don't deeply *feel* the disappointment or the journey through it.

## Bottom Line:
This is solid, competent work that fulfills the assignment. But for two months of revision, I'd expect more depth in the second half and a more developed emotional arc. It's good - just not quite as polished as the timeline suggests it could be.
